CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Mar-08-2024 Continued To May-08-24 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-15-2024 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT POLYCULTURE RESEARCH LLC's DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Polyculture Research LLC's demurrer to plaintiff Fast AF Technologies, Inc.'s complaint is overruled.

In 2022, Polyculture agreed to provide Fast AF with data and statistics services. (Compl. para. 8.) The parties executed a written agreement and statement of work ("SOW"). (Compl. para. 8.) Polyculture allegedly breached the agreement by failing to obtain approval for certain decisions it made. (Compl. para. 12.) Fast AF sued for breach of contract, unjust enrichment, and breach of implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Polyculture now demurs to all three causes of action.

First, Polyculture argues the entire complaint fails for indefiniteness because Fast AF did not allege precisely how Polyculture transgressed. (CCP 430.10(f).) "Demurrers for uncertainty are disfavored and are granted only if the pleading is so incomprehensible that a defendant cannot reasonably respond." (Morris v. JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A. (2022) 78 Cal.App.5th 279, 292.) Here, the complaint attaches the agreement and identifies specific ways in which Polyculture breached, i.e., "by purportedly providing services that went beyond the scope of the SOW without Plaintiff's prior written consent and failing to obtain Plaintiff's prior written consent as to Defendant's personnel pursuant to the terms of the Agreements." (Compl. para. 16.) These allegations are sufficiently detailed, and Polyculture can reasonably respond.

Second, Polyculture argues unjust enrichment is a general principle, not a valid cause of action. (See Melchior v. New Line Productions, Inc. (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 779, 793.) Even if unjust enrichment does not describe a cause of action, it is synonymous with restitution, which does. (O'Grady v. Merchant Exchange Productions, Inc. (2019) 41 Cal.App.5th 771, 792.) At minimum, Fast AF has pled a valid claim for restitution, despite the label of unjust enrichment. The claim is not duplicative but a permissible, alternate theory of recovery. (See Adams v. Paul (1995) 11 Cal.4th 583, 592 [factually or legally inconsistent allegations may be pled in the alternative].)

Third, Polyculture argues the breach of implied covenant claim fails as duplicative. Allegations that merely restate contract breach, rely on the same alleged acts, and seek the same damages may be disregarded as superfluous. (See Careau & Co. v. Security Pacific Business Credit, Inc. (1990) 222 Cal.App.3d 1371, 1395, 1401.) While Fast AF's third cause of action mirrors the first, the third cause of action does include two unique facts to support unfair interference, i.e., overbilling and failing to resolve billing disputes in good faith. (Compl. para. 27.) The claim is not wholly duplicative. Liberally construing the complaint, Fast AF has stated a viable breach of implied covenant claim.

Order signed.

Judge: Rochelle C. East; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not Reported. (302/RCE)

MINI MINUTES:

RE: DEFENDANT POLYCULTURE RESEARCH LLC's DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Off calendar. Declaration of Jeremy Wang indicates parties did not meet and confer by in-person, by phone, or by video conference, as required by CCP 430.31. Parties are ordered to meet and confer immediately. The responsive pleading is now due by January 13, 2024.
